name: M1
desc: The Crab Nebula — remnant of a supernova observed by Chinese astronomers in 1054 AD. A pulsar at its heart spins 30 times per second.

name: M2
desc: One of the richest and most compact globular clusters, containing ~150,000 stars at 37,000 light-years.

name: M3
desc: A spectacular globular cluster with ~500,000 stars. One of the finest in the northern sky.

name: M4
desc: One of the closest globular clusters at ~7,200 light-years, containing ancient white dwarf stars.

name: M5
desc: A brilliant globular cluster, one of the oldest known at ~13 billion years old.

name: M6
desc: The Butterfly Cluster — a young open cluster whose brightest star is an orange supergiant.

name: M7
desc: Ptolemy's Cluster — visible to the naked eye, one of the most prominent open clusters in Scorpius.

name: M8
desc: The Lagoon Nebula — a giant interstellar cloud where new stars are actively forming.

name: M9
desc: A globular cluster near the galactic core, heavily obscured by interstellar dust.

name: M10
desc: A bright globular cluster at ~14,300 light-years, relatively close with a loose core.

name: M11
desc: The Wild Duck Cluster — one of the richest and most compact open clusters known.

name: M12
desc: A loose globular cluster that may have lost many of its small stars to the Milky Way's gravity.

name: M13
desc: The Great Hercules Cluster — the finest globular cluster in the northern sky with ~300,000 stars.

name: M14
desc: A large globular cluster that hosted a nova in 1938, discovered on old photographic plates.

name: M15
desc: One of the most densely packed globulars, possibly containing a central black hole.

name: M16
desc: The Eagle Nebula — home to the famous 'Pillars of Creation' star-forming columns photographed by Hubble.

name: M17
desc: The Omega (Swan) Nebula — a bright emission nebula and active star-forming region.

name: M18
desc: A young, sparse open cluster embedded in the Milky Way, about 4,900 light-years away.

name: M19
desc: A slightly oblate globular cluster — one of the most oblate known, flattened by tidal forces.

name: M20
desc: The Trifid Nebula — a rare combination of emission, reflection, and dark nebulae divided into three lobes.

name: M21
desc: A young open cluster near the Trifid Nebula, just 4 million years old.

name: M22
desc: A brilliant globular cluster near the galactic core, one of the nearest at ~10,400 light-years.

name: M23
desc: A rich open cluster about 2,150 light-years away in Sagittarius.

name: M24
desc: The Sagittarius Star Cloud — not a true cluster but a dense window through the Milky Way dust.

name: M25
desc: A large, bright open cluster visible to the naked eye in a rich Milky Way field.

name: M26
desc: A compact open cluster about 5,000 light-years away in Scutum.

name: M27
desc: The Dumbbell Nebula — the first planetary nebula ever discovered (1764), with a striking hourglass shape.

name: M28
desc: A compact globular cluster near M22 that was the first to be shown to contain a millisecond pulsar.

name: M29
desc: A small, young open cluster near Sadr in Cygnus, embedded in bright nebulosity.

name: M30
desc: A compact globular with a dense, concentrated core; shows evidence of core collapse.

name: M31
desc: The Andromeda Galaxy — our nearest large galactic neighbor at 2.5 million light-years, on a collision course with the Milky Way.

name: M32
desc: A compact elliptical satellite galaxy of Andromeda, one of the closest ellipticals to Earth.

name: M33
desc: The Triangulum Galaxy — the third-largest Local Group member, barely visible to the naked eye under dark skies.

name: M34
desc: A bright open cluster of ~100 stars easily resolved in binoculars.

name: M35
desc: A rich open cluster with a fainter, more distant cluster (NGC 2158) visible in the same field.

name: M36
desc: One of three young open clusters (with M36, M37, M38) embedded in the Auriga Milky Way.

name: M37
desc: The richest of the three Auriga open clusters, containing over 500 stars.

name: M38
desc: An open cluster containing ~100 stars in a cross-like shape within Auriga.

name: M39
desc: A large, scattered open cluster of about 30 bright stars, relatively close at ~825 light-years.

name: M40
desc: Winnecke 4 — a visual double star included in Messier's catalog by mistake; not a true nebula.

name: M41
desc: A large open cluster just 4° south of Sirius, visible to the naked eye.

name: M42
desc: The Orion Nebula — the most spectacular and studied stellar nursery, 1,344 light-years away.

name: M43
desc: De Mairan's Nebula — a comma-shaped nebula separated from the Orion Nebula by a dark lane.

name: M44
desc: The Beehive Cluster (Praesepe) — one of the nearest open clusters, visible to the naked eye.

name: M45
desc: The Pleiades — the famous Seven Sisters open cluster, 440 light-years away, wrapped in blue reflection nebulosity.

name: M46
desc: A rich open cluster containing ~500 stars plus a foreground planetary nebula (NGC 2438).

name: M47
desc: A young, bright open cluster of ~50 stars, just 1,600 light-years away.

name: M48
desc: A large open cluster of ~80 stars missed by Messier due to a position error, later identified.

name: M49
desc: The brightest member of the Virgo Cluster of galaxies, an elliptical at ~56 million light-years.

name: M50
desc: An open cluster in Monoceros with about 200 stars, one reddish giant prominent at its center.

name: M51
desc: The Whirlpool Galaxy — a grand-design spiral galaxy interacting with its companion NGC 5195, 23 million light-years away.

name: M52
desc: A rich open cluster of ~200 stars embedded in the Milky Way in Cassiopeia.

name: M53
desc: A remote globular cluster at ~58,000 light-years, one of the most distant Messier globulars.

name: M54
desc: A globular cluster that belongs not to the Milky Way but to the Sagittarius Dwarf Galaxy.

name: M55
desc: A large, loose globular cluster with very little central concentration.

name: M56
desc: A loose globular cluster in Lyra, between M57 and Albireo.

name: M57
desc: The Ring Nebula — a classic planetary nebula, the glowing shell ejected by a dying star.

name: M58
desc: One of the brightest galaxies in the Virgo Cluster, a barred spiral at 62 million light-years.

name: M59
desc: An elliptical galaxy in Virgo, one of the larger ellipticals in the Virgo Cluster.

name: M60
desc: One of the largest elliptical galaxies known, interacting with spiral NGC 4647.

name: M61
desc: A face-on spiral galaxy in Virgo, one of the largest members of the Virgo Cluster.

name: M62
desc: One of the most asymmetric globular clusters, deformed by the galactic bulge's tidal forces.

name: M63
desc: The Sunflower Galaxy — a flocculent spiral with a pattern of disconnected spiral arms.

name: M64
desc: The Black Eye Galaxy — a spiral galaxy with a dark dust lane absorbing light in front of its nucleus.

name: M65
desc: A spiral galaxy in Leo, one of the Leo Triplet along with M66 and NGC 3628.

name: M66
desc: The largest of the Leo Triplet, a spiral galaxy with an asymmetric shape from tidal interactions.

name: M67
desc: One of the oldest known open clusters at ~4 billion years, still coherent despite its age.

name: M68
desc: A globular cluster in Hydra approaching the Milky Way at high velocity.

name: M69
desc: A compact globular cluster near the galactic center in Sagittarius.

name: M70
desc: A compact globular with a collapsed core, similar to M69 in the same Sagittarius field.

name: M71
desc: An unusual object debated between globular and open cluster; now classified globular.

name: M72
desc: A distant, relatively faint globular cluster in Aquarius, ~53,000 light-years away.

name: M73
desc: An asterism of four unrelated stars included by Messier; not a true cluster.

name: M74
desc: A face-on grand-design spiral galaxy with very low surface brightness, challenging to observe.

name: M75
desc: One of the most remote and concentrated globular clusters in the Messier catalog.

name: M76
desc: The Little Dumbbell Nebula — a complex bipolar planetary nebula in Perseus.

name: M77
desc: A barred spiral Seyfert galaxy with an active nucleus, one of the largest Messier galaxies.

name: M78
desc: The brightest diffuse reflection nebula in the sky, illuminated by two hot stars.

name: M79
desc: An unusual globular cluster in Lepus, possibly captured from the Canis Major Dwarf Galaxy.

name: M80
desc: A dense globular cluster that hosted a bright nova (T Sco) in 1860.

name: M81
desc: Bode's Galaxy — a grand-design spiral and brightest Messier galaxy visible from mid-northern latitudes.

name: M82
desc: The Cigar Galaxy — a starburst galaxy with a superwind of ionized gas exploding from its center.

name: M83
desc: The Southern Pinwheel — one of the nearest and brightest spiral galaxies, hosting several historic supernovae.

name: M84
desc: An elliptical (or lenticular) galaxy in the core of the Virgo Cluster.

name: M85
desc: The northernmost Virgo Cluster galaxy in Messier's catalog, an interacting lenticular.

name: M86
desc: A giant elliptical near M84 moving toward us at 244 km/s — an infalling Virgo Cluster member.

name: M87
desc: Virgo A — a giant elliptical galaxy famous for its jet and the first black hole ever imaged (Event Horizon Telescope, 2019).

name: M88
desc: A multi-arm spiral galaxy in the Virgo Cluster showing a slight asymmetry.

name: M89
desc: A nearly perfectly spherical elliptical galaxy in the Virgo Cluster.

name: M90
desc: The only Messier galaxy with a blueshift — it is approaching us within the Virgo Cluster.

name: M91
desc: A barred spiral galaxy in Coma Berenices, missing from catalogs for nearly 200 years.

name: M92
desc: A bright globular cluster often overlooked near its famous neighbor M13 in Hercules.

name: M93
desc: A bright open cluster of ~80 stars in Puppis, about 3,600 light-years away.

name: M94
desc: A spiral galaxy with two ring structures, the inner one a powerful starburst zone.

name: M95
desc: A barred spiral galaxy in Leo with a prominent circular bar and ring structure.

name: M96
desc: The brightest galaxy in the M96 Group, an asymmetric spiral with an off-center nucleus.

name: M97
desc: The Owl Nebula — a planetary nebula with two dark eye-like patches, 2,030 light-years away.

name: M98
desc: A nearly edge-on spiral galaxy moving toward us — one of the few blueshifted Messier galaxies.

name: M99
desc: A near-perfect face-on spiral galaxy in Coma Berenices, rotating rapidly and somewhat isolated.

name: M100
desc: A grand-design spiral in the Virgo Cluster, one of the brightest members at 55 million light-years.

name: M101
desc: The Pinwheel Galaxy — a face-on spiral with striking asymmetry and many prominent HII regions.

name: M102
desc: Disputed identity; likely a re-observation of M101 or NGC 5866 (Spindle Galaxy).

name: M103
desc: A young open cluster near the Double Cluster, one of the most distant Messier clusters at ~8,500 ly.

name: M104
desc: The Sombrero Galaxy — an edge-on spiral with a dramatic dust lane and large central bulge.

name: M105
desc: An elliptical galaxy in Leo, one of the first galaxies known to harbor a central supermassive black hole.

name: M106
desc: A spiral galaxy with two anomalous spiral arms composed of hot gas rather than stars.

name: M107
desc: A loose, sparse globular cluster in Ophiuchus, the last Messier object discovered in his lifetime.

name: M108
desc: An edge-on barred spiral in Ursa Major, near M97 in the same field.

name: M109
desc: A barred spiral galaxy in Ursa Major with a classic theta (θ) bar-and-ring morphology.

name: M110
desc: The outermost Messier satellite of the Andromeda Galaxy, a dwarf elliptical showing subtle dust lanes.
